questions.question: Why is an autonomous coding agent on your machine a distinct security category from a chatbot?
options: It uses more electricity
Its attack surface is everything it can reach — filesystem, network, credentials, clipboard, terminals — a new risk profile, not a chatbot feature update
It cannot be uninstalled
It always runs as administrator
questions.explanation: Schneier and others flag this publicly: computer-use agents act on real state. That's why Claude Code ships a seven-mode permission ladder instead of one autonomous/not-autonomous switch.

questions.question: Across the permission ladder, what distinguishes plan, acceptEdits, and bypassPermissions?
options: They select different models

plan reviews every action against an approved plan; acceptEdits auto-approves file writes but still confirms shell/network; bypassPermissions approves everything and belongs only in throwaway containers
They differ only in pricing tier
plan is read-only; the others are identical
questions.explanation: Each rung trades review-per-action for speed. The documented placement: plan for unfamiliar/prod-adjacent work, acceptEdits for big refactors, bypassPermissions "only inside ephemeral containers you are willing to throw away."

questions.question: How does Auto Mode's two-stage classifier work?
options: Two humans review every action
A cheap single-token classifier runs on every action in parallel; flagged actions get a chain-of-thought policy review that can escalate to the user
Stage 1 encrypts, stage 2 decrypts
It votes between two different models on every action
questions.explanation: Stage 1 keeps approved actions off the user's critical path; stage 2 does the focused safety assessment over action, session state, and declared task — escalating to human review at low confidence. Budgets (max_turns, max_budget_usd, per-tool caps) sit alongside.

questions.question: Which failure shape can per-action classification structurally miss?
options: Misspelled shell commands
Compositions of individually-safe actions — e.g. writing data to your own file then git-pushing to a public repo; each step is allowed, the sequence is exfiltration
Actions in languages other than English
Actions that take longer than a second
questions.explanation: The classifier judges the action, not the trajectory's intent. Subtle injection that never produces one flagged action, and semantic-level misbehavior across steps, are the documented gaps — which is why Anthropic states the classifier is not sufficient alone.

questions.question: What preconditions does the lesson set for running autoMode unattended?
options: A paid subscription and a fast GPU
A workspace with measured blast radius: no credentials, no production mounts, no egress you didn't opt into — plus budgets and trajectory audits
At least 100 prior supervised runs
Nothing — autoMode is fully safe by design
questions.explanation: Auto Mode is a research preview and a layer, not a solution: combine it with allowlists, isolation, budget caps, and audits. The eval-vs-deploy gap from Lesson 1 applies to safety classifiers too.
